methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 3 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Archer Aviation
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Public), post-money $5B

data.narrative:
Joby Aviation operates in the Drones & Autonomous sector as a publicly traded company with a current valuation exceeding $10B, having raised over $2B to date. The addressable peer universe for valuation purposes is constrained: the Drones & Autonomous sector contains six identifiable private comparables—including Archer Aviation, Nuro, Elroy Air, Aurora Innovation, and ideaForge—but no public peers in the same or adjacent development stages. This structural limitation in comparable company availability reflects the nascent state of commercialized autonomous aircraft.

Standard peer-median triangulation is not applicable to this valuation. The narrow peer set lacks sufficient depth: only one same-stage comparable exists within the Drones & Autonomous sector, with zero adjacent-stage peers and two similar-tier companies. Deriving a meaningful multiple from fewer than three datapoints risks statistical distortion. An all-sector median approach would introduce unacceptable cross-sector bias given material differences in capital intensity, regulatory pathways, and go-to-market timelines between autonomous aircraft and adjacent drone categories.

The absence of a robust public comp baseline and the thinness of the private peer set create meaningful valuation opacity. Any analysis would require either: (1) explicit multi-method triangulation incorporating precedent transaction data or DCF modeling; (2) qualitative assessment of stage-specific risk premiums; or (3) acceptance of wide valuation ranges pending market maturation and IPO clustering in the sector. Current market pricing above $10B should be stress-tested against regulatory milestones and pathway-to-revenue timelines rather than reliant on comparable multiples.
